figures.caption: Fig. 2 Velocity magnitude evolution at 𝑥 = 200 for Re = 1000. Each column corresponds to one time snapshot, and color indicates velocity magnitude.

Fig. 17 Relative reconstruction error for all testing cases in the beam benchmark. Both methods achieve low error, while cXGBoost consistently yields smaller reconstruction error across the parameter domain.
Fig. 18 Distribution of relative reconstruction errors across all folds. Both methods show comparable interquartile ranges, while cXGBoost exhibits fewer high-error outliers.

Fig. 7 Simulation setup and wave evolution for the double-slit experiment. A 40 × 40 triangular CG2 mesh (6,561 DOFs) discretizes the unit square domain. Two slits (S1, S2) on the left boundary act as time-harmonic sources, generating waves that propagate across the domain under Neumann boundary conditions. Snapshots show the wave evolution over time.

Fig. 13 Space–time evolution of the Burgers solution for (𝑎, 𝜈) = (1.0, 0.0200). The solution exhibits a parameter-dependent transport–diffusion structure that leads to a nonlinear evolution of the associated reduced subspaces.

Fig. 14 Relative reconstruction error for all testing cases in the one-dimensional Burgers benchmark. The interpolation baseline shows greater variability across the parameter domain, while cXGBoost maintains more consistent prediction accuracy.
Fig. 1 Velocity magnitude field for Re = 1000 at time step 𝑡 = 16,000. The red line at 𝑥 = 200 marks the slice used to extract velocity profiles for ROM training.
Fig. 6 Distribution of relative errors from 5-fold cross-validation. cXGBoost achieves lower median error and reduced variance, while interpolation suffers from wide dispersion and high outliers.

Fig. 11 Distribution of relative reconstruction errors from 5-fold cross-validation for the wave propagation example. Both methods achieve low reconstruction error, while cXGBoost exhibits a more compact distribution and reduced variance across folds.
Fig. 10 Worst-case reconstruction example for the interpolation baseline at (𝜇1, 𝜇2) = (96, 5.0).

Fig. 15 Distribution of relative reconstruction errors from 5-fold cross-validation for the Burgers example. Both methods achieve low reconstruction error, with cXGBoost showing slightly lower median error and fewer high-error outliers.
Fig. 4 Reconstruction at Re = 200. Both methods are accurate at low Re.
Fig. 5 Reconstruction at Re = 1200. The learning-based model preserves coherent vortex patterns even in nonlinear regimes.

Fig. 8 Relative reconstruction error for all testing cases in the wave propagation benchmark. The interpolation baseline exhibits larger fluctuations in error, whereas cXGBoost maintains consistently low reconstruction error across the parameter domain.

Fig. 9 Best-case reconstruction example for the double-slit problem at (𝜇1, 𝜇2) = (80, 4.2). Both interpolation and cXGBoost accurately recover the dominant POD mode, with cXGBoost achieving slightly lower error.
Fig. 3 Reconstruction error as a function of Reynolds number. The interpolation baseline diverges at high Re, whereas cXGBoost remains stable and accurate.
Table 6 Hyperparameters for the cXGBoost model across all case studies.
Fig. 16 Representative beam response for (𝜇1, 𝜇2) = (5, 7). The solution exhibits smooth oscillatory behavior with structured temporal evolution induced by dual-frequency forcing.

Fig. 12 Representative one-dimensional Burgers solution profiles for (𝑎, 𝜈) = (1.0, 0.0200) at several time instances. The initially smooth waveform progressively deforms under nonlinear advection while viscosity smooths steep gradients.
